statusLine: European fuel security is under strain with 19 of 27 countries showing critical levels in at least one fuel type, while oil prices remain elevated at $93.09/barrel following recent strategic reserve releases.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel stocks are showing significant pressure across multiple countries and fuel types as of March 2026. While EU average reserves stand at 96.3 days for petrol, 88.8 days for diesel, and 87.3 days for jet fuel, the distribution reveals concerning vulnerabilities. Diesel stocks are particularly tight, averaging below the 90-day mandatory minimum at 88.8 days, with 19 countries falling short of this threshold.

The situation is most acute for smaller economies and Eastern European nations. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania face critical shortages across all three fuel categories, with Romania's diesel reserves at just 30.2 days—the lowest in the dataset. Even larger economies show strain: France has critical petrol levels at 69.3 days, while Germany's jet fuel sits at 59.9 days. The widespread nature of these shortages suggests systemic pressure rather than isolated national issues.

Fuel prices reflect this supply tension, with the EU average reaching €1.812 for petrol and €1.821 for diesel. The 28.4% surge in Brent crude from $72.48 to $93.09 since late February underscores the market response to Middle East disruptions and subsequent strategic reserve releases. This price environment, combined with depleted stocks, leaves Europe's fuel security under significant strain heading into the summer driving season.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries have critical fuel levels in at least one category, with diesel particularly affected across the bloc
EU diesel reserves average 88.8 days, falling below the mandatory 90-day minimum for net imports
Romania faces the most severe shortage with diesel at just 30.2 days, while Luxembourg shows critical levels across all fuel types
Oil prices have surged 28.4% since late February to $93.09/barrel, driving fuel costs to €1.81+ per litre across the EU
Major economies including France, Germany, and Italy all show critical shortages in key fuel categories
